md: Every non-vertical line in the plane can be written using just **two numbers**: how steep it is, and where it crosses the y-axis. That's the entire idea behind **slope-intercept form**.
Here, $m$ is the **slope** and $b$ is the **y-intercept**. Once you know $m$ and $b$, you know the line.
The visualization below lets you change $m$ and $b$ and watch the line respond. Drag both sliders — try to get a feel for what each one controls.
Slope tells you **how steep** the line is, and **which way it tilts**. Formally, slope is *rise over run*: if you move 1 unit to the right, how many units up do you go?

If $m > 0$, the line goes **up** as you read left to right. If $m < 0$, it goes **down**. If $m = 0$, the line is **flat** (horizontal). The bigger the absolute value of $m$, the **steeper** the line.

blocks.title: Try it
blocks.body: In the widget above, set $m = 2$ then $m = 0.5$. The line with $m = 2$ climbs *faster*. For every step right, it goes up twice as far.

md: The number $b$ is the **y-intercept** — the y-value where the line crosses the vertical axis. It's where the line is when $x = 0$.
Plug in $x = 0$ and you get $y = m(0) + b = b$. So the line *always* passes through the point $(0, b)$.
Changing $b$ slides the whole line up or down without changing its tilt.

blocks.title: Spot the y-intercept
blocks.body: In the widget, the **blue dot** is $(0, b)$. As you drag the $b$ slider, watch that dot — and the whole line — slide up and down.

md: ### Reading a line from its equation
Given $y = mx + b$, you can read off the slope and y-intercept directly:
If a line passes through two points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$, the slope is the change in $y$ divided by the change in $x$:
Drag the two points below to see this formula in action. The widget computes the slope live, then plugs it back into $y = mx + b$ to draw the line.

blocks.title: Special cases
blocks.body: **Horizontal lines** have $m = 0$ — the equation is just $y = b$. **Vertical lines** can't be written as $y = mx + b$ at all (the slope is undefined); they're written as $x = c$ for some constant $c$.

md: Slope-intercept is the most popular form, but two others come up often:

Each form has its strengths — point-slope is convenient when you know one point and the slope; standard form is handy for finding both intercepts quickly. We'll cover those in detail in the next subtopics.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: $y = mx + b$ where $m$ is **slope** (rise over run) and $b$ is the **y-intercept**. Slope tells you the tilt; intercept tells you where the line crosses the y-axis. Together, they define a unique line.
